docs.title: Welcome to AI Learning Path
docs.text: A comprehensive, self-contained learning resource for software engineers transitioning into AI/ML. This repository provides structured guidance from foundational concepts through production deployment.

docs.title: 🎯 What You'll Learn
docs.text:
This path will teach you:

  • Machine Learning fundamentals - Core concepts, algorithms, and best practices
  • Deep Learning - Neural networks, CNNs, RNNs, and Transformers
  • Generative AI - LLMs, Agents, RAG, and production deployment
  • Real-world skills - Data preprocessing, model evaluation, debugging, and deployment

docs.title: Option 1: Follow Your Goal (Recommended)
docs.text:
Know what you want to build? Use our Learning Paths by Goal:

  • 🤖 Build Chatbots → 3-4 weeks
  • 📊 Build Recommendation Systems → 6-8 weeks
  • 👁️ Build Computer Vision Apps → 7-9 weeks
  • 📝 Build NLP Applications → 6-8 weeks
  • 🎯 Build Autonomous Agents → 8-10 weeks
  • 🎓 Full ML/AI Engineering Career → 4-5 months

docs.title: Option 2: Follow the Structured Path
docs.text:
Prefer a comprehensive, sequential path? Follow this order:

  1. Prerequisites - Setup your environment
  2. Foundations - Math and key concepts
  3. Machine Learning - Core ML fundamentals
  4. Deep Learning - Neural networks and architectures
  5. Gen AI Learning Path - Modern LLMs and applications
  6. Production Deployment - Deploy to production

docs.title: Option 3: Jump to Specific Topics
docs.text:
Already know some background? Jump directly to:

  • Machine Learning Fundamentals
  • Deep Learning Beginner Section
  • Generative AI Basics
  • RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
  • Agents
  • MCP (Model Context Protocol)

docs.title: Foundations
docs.text:
• Math Essentials - Linear algebra, calculus, probability for ML (intuition-focused, not rigorous)
  • Glossary - Quick reference for ML/AI terminology
  • Learning Paths by Goal - Six different learning paths based on what you want to build

docs.title: Core Learning
docs.text:
• Machine Learning - Supervised/unsupervised learning, algorithms, evaluation metrics
  • Deep Learning - Neural networks, CNNs, RNNs, Transformers (Beginner → Advanced)
  • Gen AI Path - LLMs, prompt engineering, vector databases

docs.title: Specialized Topics
docs.text:
• RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) - Ground LLMs in real data
  • Agents - Multi-step reasoning and tool use
  • MCP - Standard protocol for AI tool integration

docs.title: Support & Deployment
docs.text:
• Common Issues & Solutions - Debugging guide and troubleshooting
  • Production Deployment - APIs, Docker, cloud hosting, monitoring

docs.title: 🛠️ Learning Approach
docs.text:
This repository uses:

  1. Conceptual depth - Understand why and when, not just how
  2. Structured progression - Beginner → Intermediate → Advanced per section
  3. Practical focus - Real code examples, projects, and deployment
  4. Self-contained - Minimal external resource dependency
  5. Curated resources - Best external learning materials when needed

docs.title: 📋 Before You Start
docs.text:
Ensure you have:

  • Python experience - Basic programming knowledge
  • Environment setup - See Prerequisites
  • Time commitment - 3-5 months for comprehensive path (or 3-4 weeks for specific goal)
  • Curiosity - AI/ML requires experimentation and patience!

docs.title: 💡 Recommended Starting Point
docs.text:
If you're unsure where to start:

  1. Read Prerequisites (15 min)
  2. Check Glossary if any terms are unfamiliar
  3. Go to Learning Paths by Goal and find your interest
  4. Start learning!

docs.title: 🎓 Learning Tips
docs.text:
1. Do projects, not just read - Hands-on coding is essential
  2. Start simple - Begin with small models and datasets
  3. Debug systematically - Check Common Issues when stuck
  4. Iterate - ML is experimental; try variations
  5. Join communities - Reddit's r/MachineLearning, Discord, GitHub Discussions

docs.title: 🔄 When You're Done
docs.text:
After completing a learning path:

  • Build a portfolio project combining multiple concepts
  • Share your work on GitHub or blogs
  • Join communities to stay current as the field evolves
  • Specialize in an area of interest (NLP, Computer Vision, Agents, etc.)

docs.text:

  3. Review Prerequisites to verify setup
  4. Refer to relevant section (ML, DL, Gen AI) for detailed explanations

docs.title: Common Issues & Solutions
docs.text: This page addresses common problems, errors, and conceptual confusions that learners encounter across the AI/ML learning path. Check here if you're stuck!

docs.title: Issue: "ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'X'"
docs.text:
Problem: You're trying to import a library that isn't installed.

Solutions:

  1. Check if package is installed:
pip list | grep package_name
  1. Install the missing package:
pip install package_name
  1. For common ML libraries:
pip install numpy pandas scikit-learn matplotlib
pip install torch torchvision  # PyTorch
pip install tensorflow keras   # TensorFlow
pip install langchain huggingface-hub  # Gen AI
  1. Restart your Jupyter kernel after installing:
  2. In Jupyter: Kernel → Restart

  3. In VS Code: Restart Kernel button

  4. Check Python environment:

  5. Ensure you're using the right Python environment (especially with virtual envs/conda)
  6. In Jupyter: Check kernel name (top-right corner)

docs.title: Issue: "Permission denied" or "No module named pip"
docs.text:
Problem: pip isn't accessible or you lack permissions.

Solutions:

  1. Use explicit Python module:
python3 -m pip install package_name
  1. For permission issues (macOS/Linux):
pip install --user package_name
  1. Use a virtual environment (recommended):
python3 -m venv env
source env/bin/activate  # macOS/Linux
env\Scripts\activate     # Windows
pip install package_name

docs.title: Issue: GPU not being used (PyTorch/TensorFlow)
docs.text:
Problem: Training is slow; you expected GPU acceleration.

Solutions:

  1. Check if GPU is available:
import torch
print(torch.cuda.is_available())      # PyTorch
print(torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))  # Device name
  1. For TensorFlow:
import tensorflow as tf
print(tf.config.list_physical_devices('GPU'))
  1. Install GPU drivers:
  2. NVIDIA CUDA Toolkit

  3. cuDNN library

  4. Use Google Colab (easier):

  5. Go to Runtime → Change runtime type → GPU
  6. Free tier provides access to GPUs

docs.title: Issue: "Port already in use" or Jupyter won't start
docs.text:
Problem: Can't launch Jupyter notebook.

Solutions:

  1. Kill process using the port:
# Find process using port 8888
lsof -i :8888  # macOS/Linux
# Kill the process
kill -9 <PID>
  1. Use a different port:
jupyter notebook --port 8889
  1. Use VS Code instead:
  2. Easier, no port conflicts
  3. Built-in cell execution

docs.title: Issue: Model accuracy is very low (< 50% for classification)
docs.text:
Diagnosis & Solutions:

  1. Check data quality:
# Look at your data
df.head()
df.isnull().sum()  # Missing values?
df.describe()      # Value ranges make sense?
  1. Check train-test split:
  2. Are train and test distributions similar?

  3. Is test data properly separated?

  4. Simple baseline first:

  5. Start with simple model (logistic regression, decision tree)

  6. If baseline fails, problem is likely data quality, not model complexity

  7. Check feature scaling:

from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
  1. Wrong preprocessing:
  2. Fit scaler on training data only
  3. Apply same scaler to test data
# CORRECT
scaler = StandardScaler().fit(X_train)
X_train_scaled = scaler.transform(X_train)
X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)  # Use fitted scaler!

docs.title: Issue: Model overfits (high train accuracy, low test accuracy)
docs.text:
Problem: Model memorizes training data but doesn't generalize.

Solutions:

  1. Get more data:

  2. Overfitting common with small datasets

  3. Add regularization:

# Scikit-learn
LogisticRegression(C=1.0, penalty='l2')  # L2 regularization

# Reduce model complexity
DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5)  # Limit tree depth
  1. Use cross-validation:
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5)
print(f"Mean: {scores.mean()}, Std: {scores.std()}")
  1. Early stopping (neural networks):

  2. Stop training when validation loss stops improving

  3. Data augmentation:

  4. Artificially increase dataset size (rotate images, paraphrase text)

docs.title: Issue: "ConvergenceWarning" or model won't train
docs.text:
Problem: Optimization algorithm couldn't converge.

Solutions:

  1. Increase max iterations:
LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000)  # Default is 100
  1. Reduce learning rate or scale features:
# Scale features first!
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
X = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)
  1. Check for NaN/infinite values:
import numpy as np
print(np.isnan(X).sum())
print(np.isinf(X).sum())

docs.title: Issue: "Remember to fit before transform"
docs.text:
Problem: Applying transformation that hasn't been fitted on training data.

Correct pattern:

# CORRECT
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
scaler = StandardScaler()
scaler.fit(X_train)      # Learn from training data
X_train = scaler.transform(X_train)
X_test = scaler.transform(X_test)  # Apply learned transformation

# WRONG - Don't fit on combined data!
scaler.fit(np.concatenate([X_train, X_test]))  # Data leakage!

docs.title: Issue: "CUDA out of memory" or "RuntimeError: CUDA out of memory"
docs.text:
Problem: Model/batch too large for GPU memory.

Solutions:

  1. Reduce batch size:
batch_size = 32  # Reduce from 128 or 256
dataloader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=batch_size)
  1. Use gradient accumulation:
# Simulate larger batch without more memory
for i, (x, y) in enumerate(dataloader):
    loss = model(x, y)
    loss.backward()
    if (i + 1) % 4 == 0:  # Accumulate 4 batches
        optimizer.step()
  1. Use mixed precision training:
from torch.cuda.amp import autocast
with autocast():
    outputs = model(inputs)
  1. Use CPU (slower but works):
device = 'cpu'  # Instead of 'cuda'
model.to(device)
  1. Use smaller model:
  2. Try MobileNet instead of ResNet152
  3. Reduce hidden layer sizes

docs.title: Issue: Training loss doesn't decrease / Loss becomes NaN
docs.text:
Problem: Unstable training, usually gradient-related.

Solutions:

  1. Reduce learning rate:
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)  # Lower lr
  1. Use gradient clipping:
torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
  1. Check for NaN inputs:
print(X.isnan().sum())
print(y.isnan().sum())
  1. Normalize inputs:
# Images should be in [0, 1] or [-1, 1]
X = X / 255.0  # If pixel values are 0-255
  1. Batch normalization helps:
nn.BatchNorm2d(64)  # After convolutional layers

docs.title: Issue: Model training too slowly
docs.text:
Problem: Training takes forever.

Solutions:

  1. Use GPU: (See GPU setup above)

  2. Reduce model size:

  3. Fewer layers, smaller hidden dimensions

  4. Use pre-trained models (transfer learning):

model = torchvision.models.resnet18(pretrained=True)
# Only train new head, freeze backbone
  1. Increase batch size:
  2. Faster if your GPU can handle it (30-50% speedup)

  3. But may hurt convergence

  4. Check for bottlenecks:

import cProfile
cProfile.run('train_one_epoch()')

docs.title: Issue: "API rate limit exceeded"
docs.text:
Problem: You've made too many requests to free LLM API.

Solutions:

  1. Wait and retry:
  2. Most services reset limits hourly or daily
import time
time.sleep(60)  # Wait 1 minute
  1. Switch to free API:
  2. Try Google Gemini, Groq, or Together AI

  3. Each has different rate limits

  4. Use local LLM:

# Llama.cpp - run locally, no rate limits
pip install llama-cpp-python
  1. Batch requests:
  2. Fewer API calls per request

docs.title: Issue: LLM response is irrelevant or "hallucinated"
docs.text:
Problem: Model generates plausible but false information.

Solutions:

  1. Use RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation):
  2. Ground responses in real documents

  3. See RAG guide

  4. Better prompt engineering:

  5. Give specific instructions
  6. Use examples (few-shot prompting)

  7. Ask for reasoning step-by-step

  8. Lower temperature:

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-3.5",
    messages=[...],
    temperature=0.2  # Lower = more deterministic
)
  1. Use function calling/tools:
  2. Let agent decide which tool to use
  3. Prevents hallucinated responses

docs.title: Issue: "Max tokens exceeded" or context window full
docs.text:
Problem: Your prompt is too long for the model.

Solutions:

  1. Summarize context:
  2. Don't send entire documents

  3. Extract relevant sections first

  4. Use RAG with chunking:

  5. Break documents into chunks

  6. Retrieve only relevant chunks

  7. Use model with larger context:

  8. GPT-4 Turbo: 128K tokens
  9. Claude 3 Opus: 200K tokens
  10. Open source: Check model specs

docs.title: Issue: "Invalid API key" or authentication error
docs.text:
Problem: Can't authenticate with LLM API.

Solutions:

  1. Check credentials:
import os
print(os.getenv('OPENAI_API_KEY'))  # Should print your key
  1. Set environment variable correctly:
export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."  # macOS/Linux
set OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...        # Windows
  1. Use .env file (recommended):
pip install python-dotenv

from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # Loads from .env file
  1. Never commit API keys:
  2. Add to .gitignore
  3. Use environment variables or .env files

docs.title: Issue: "It works locally but fails in production"
docs.text:
Common causes & fixes:

  1. Different data format:
  2. Test on unseen data, not training data

  3. Check data types (int vs. float, string encoding)

  4. Version mismatch:

pip freeze > requirements.txt  # Save versions locally
pip install -r requirements.txt  # Use same versions in prod
  1. Missing preprocessing:
  2. Always apply same preprocessing to new data

  3. Save scaler/encoder, load in production

  4. Environment variables:

  5. API keys, model paths might be different
  6. Use configuration files

docs.title: Debugging Checklist
docs.text:
When stuck, try this systematically:

  • [ ] Check error message carefully (Google it!)
  • [ ] Verify data format and shape
  • [ ] Look at example data with .head() or .describe()
  • [ ] Simplify: Try with smaller dataset or simpler model
  • [ ] Print intermediate values
  • [ ] Read the documentation for the library
  • [ ] Check Stack Overflow or GitHub issues
  • [ ] Try a fresh Python session (kernel restart)
  • [ ] Ask in communities: Reddit r/MachineLearning, Discord servers

docs.title: Quick Reference by Error
docs.text: Error Likely Cause Solution ModuleNotFoundError Package not installed pip install package_name CUDA out of memory Batch too large Reduce batch size NaN loss Learning rate too high Reduce learning rate, normalize inputs Low accuracy Bad data or wrong features Check data quality, start with baseline Overfitting Model too complex Add regularization, get more data Rate limit exceeded Too many API calls Wait, switch API, or use local model Hallucination LLM making stuff up Use RAG, better prompts, lower temperature ConvergenceWarning Optimization stuck Increase iterations, scale features Confused about concept Missing fundamentals Check Glossary or Math Essentials

docs.title: Still Stuck?
docs.text:
1. Check the Glossary for definitions
  2. Review Math Essentials for foundational concepts
  3. Check relevant section:
  4. ML issues → Machine Learning
  5. DL issues → Deep Learning
  6. Gen AI issues → Generative AI
  7. Search Stack Overflow with your error message
  8. Post in communities: Reddit, Discord, GitHub Discussions

Remember: Everyone gets stuck. It's normal. Debugging is a key skill in AI/ML!

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This document outlines the prerequisites for diving into the AI and Machine Learning learning paths. It covers essential concepts, tools, and techniques that you should be familiar with before starting your journey.

Before diving into AI and Machine Learning, ensure you have a solid understanding of the following concepts:

  • Python and pip package management: Familiarity with Python programming and how to manage packages using pip is essential for working with various libraries and frameworks in AI and Machine Learning.

  • Google Colab or Jupyter Notebooks: These tools are commonly used for interactive coding and data analysis. They allow you to write and execute Python code in a web-based environment, making it easier to visualize data and share your work with others.

Info

I recommend using Google Colab for its ease of use and accessibility. One doesnt need to install anything locally, and it provides free access to GPUs for training models. However, if you prefer a local setup, Jupyter Notebooks is a great alternative.

docs.title: Local Setup
docs.text:
1. Python Installation: Ensure you have Python installed on your local machine. You can download it from the official Python website. Make sure to install pip, which is included with Python installations.

  2. Visual Studio Code: Download and install Visual Studio Code, a popular code editor that supports Python development. Install the Python Data Science ExtensionPack. You will be able to run Jupyter Notebooks directly in VS Code, which is a great feature for data science projects.

docs.title: Deep Learning
docs.text: Deep learning uses neural networks with multiple layers to learn hierarchical representations of data. This section builds your understanding from foundational concepts through advanced applications.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
By the end of this section, you'll be able to:

  • Build and train neural networks from scratch
  • Understand architectures for different domains (images, sequences, text)
  • Apply transfer learning for faster training
  • Debug and optimize deep learning models

docs.title: Pre-requisites
docs.text:
Before diving into deep learning, ensure you have:

  • Machine Learning fundamentals - Understanding of supervised learning, model evaluation
  • Math Essentials - Linear algebra, calculus, probability
  • Deep Learning Framework - Either PyTorch or TensorFlow
    • PyTorch (recommended): Better for research, easier debugging, excellent tutorials
    • TensorFlow: Better for production, more deployment options
  • Google Colab or Jupyter Notebooks for hands-on coding

Note

If you're new to both frameworks, start with PyTorch. It's more intuitive and widely used in research.

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:
After this section, you'll understand:

  • How neurons, weights, and biases form neural networks
  • How data flows through networks (forward pass)
  • How networks learn (backpropagation)
  • Common activation functions and when to use them

docs.title: Neurons and Layers
docs.text:
A neuron is the basic computational unit:

output = activation(dot(weights, input) + bias)

Multiple neurons form a layer. Multiple layers form a neural network.

docs.title: Activation Functions
docs.text:
Activation functions add non-linearity, allowing networks to learn complex patterns.

Function Use Case Properties ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) Hidden layers (default choice) Non-negative, fast, prevents vanishing gradients Sigmoid Binary classification output Outputs 0-1, smooth gradient Tanh Hidden layers, RNNs Outputs -1 to 1, centered around 0 Softmax Multi-class classification Outputs probability distribution Linear Regression output No transformation (y = x)

docs.title: Loss Functions
docs.text:
Loss functions measure how wrong predictions are. We minimize loss during training.

Function Use Case Mean Squared Error (MSE) Regression Cross-Entropy Classification (binary or multi-class) Binary Cross-Entropy Binary classification

docs.title: Optimization: Gradient Descent
docs.text:
Gradient descent iteratively updates weights to minimize loss:

  1. Compute gradients (how much each weight contributes to error)
  2. Move weights in opposite direction of gradient
  3. Repeat until convergence

Learning rate controls step size. Too high = unstable; too low = slow training.

docs.title: Backpropagation
docs.text: Algorithm that efficiently computes gradients for all layers using the chain rule. Modern frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow) handle this automatically.

docs.title: Overfitting and Regularization
docs.text:
Overfitting happens when models memorize training data instead of learning generalizable patterns.

Solutions:

  • Regularization (L1/L2): Add penalty for large weights
  • Dropout: Randomly disable neurons during training to prevent co-adaptation
  • Data augmentation: Artificially increase dataset variety
  • Early stopping: Stop training when validation loss increases

docs.title: Hands-on: Build Your First Neural Network
docs.text:
Project: MNIST Digit Classification

Using PyTorch, build a simple feedforward network to classify handwritten digits:

  1. Load MNIST dataset
  2. Define a network with 2-3 hidden layers
  3. Train for 10 epochs, monitor loss
  4. Evaluate on test set (aim for >95% accuracy)

Resources:

  • PyTorch Getting Started
  • Official PyTorch MNIST Tutorial
  • FastAI Practical Deep Learning (Excellent, free, beginner-friendly)

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:
After this section, you'll understand:

  • How CNNs extract spatial features from images
  • How RNNs process sequential data
  • How transformers use attention mechanisms
  • When to use each architecture

docs.title: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)
docs.text:
CNNs are specialized for image data. They use convolutions to extract local spatial patterns.

Key components:

  • Convolution: Slide filters over image to detect patterns (edges, corners, textures)
  • Pooling: Downsample while preserving important features
  • Fully connected layers: Make final predictions

Common architectures:

  • LeNet: Original CNN for digit recognition (small, simple)
  • AlexNet: Breakthrough model for ImageNet classification (2012)
  • VGG: Simple stacked architecture, easy to understand
  • ResNet: Skip connections prevent vanishing gradients (deeper networks)
  • MobileNet: Lightweight, for mobile/edge devices
  • EfficientNet: Better accuracy-efficiency trade-off

When to use: Image classification, object detection, image segmentation

docs.title: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)
docs.text:
RNNs are specialized for sequential data (text, time series, audio). They process one element at a time while maintaining hidden state.

Key idea: Output depends on current input AND previous hidden state.

Common architectures:

  • Vanilla RNN: Simple but suffers from vanishing gradients
  • LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory): Gates control information flow, handles long-term dependencies
  • GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit): Simpler than LSTM, similar performance
  • Bidirectional RNN: Processes sequence in both directions

When to use: Text classification, language modeling, time series forecasting, machine translation

docs.title: Transformers and Attention
docs.text:
Transformers use self-attention to process sequences in parallel (vs. RNNs' sequential processing).

Key advantages:

  • Faster training (parallel processing)
  • Better long-range dependencies
  • Foundation for modern LLMs (BERT, GPT, etc.)

Architecture:

  • Encoder: Processes input sequence (used in BERT)
  • Decoder: Generates output sequence (used in GPT)
  • Encoder-Decoder: Sequence-to-sequence tasks (translation, summarization)

Attention intuition: "Which parts of input are most relevant to this task?"

docs.title: Training Practices for Deep Networks
docs.text:
Data Splitting:

  • Training set: 70-80% of data
  • Validation set: 10-15% (tune hyperparameters during training)
  • Test set: 10-15% (final evaluation, untouched during training)

Optimization Techniques:

  • SGD with momentum: Smoother convergence
  • Adam: Adaptive learning rates, often default choice
  • Learning rate scheduling: Decrease learning rate over time
  • Batch normalization: Normalize layer inputs, faster training, better generalization

Monitoring Training:

  • Plot loss (should decrease over time)
  • Plot validation accuracy (should improve but may plateau)
  • Stop training if validation loss increases (overfitting)

Early Stopping:

if validation_loss hasn't improved for N epochs:
    stop training
    load best model from checkpoint

docs.title: Transfer Learning
docs.text:
Pre-trained models learned on large datasets (ImageNet, Common Crawl) capture useful features. Reuse them!

Two approaches:

  1. Fine-tuning: Train all layers on new data (small learning rate to avoid forgetting)
  2. Feature extraction: Freeze pre-trained layers, only train new head

Benefits:

  • Faster training (start with learned features)
  • Better performance (especially with limited data)
  • Reduces computational cost

docs.title: Intermediate Projects
docs.text:
Project 1: Image Classification

  • Use CNN on CIFAR-10 or custom dataset
  • Aim for >85% accuracy
  • Try transfer learning with ResNet18

Project 2: Text Classification

  • Build LSTM for sentiment analysis on IMDB or custom reviews
  • Aim for >85% accuracy
  • Compare with simpler baseline (Naive Bayes)

Project 3: Time Series Forecasting

  • Predict stock prices, weather, or electricity demand using LSTM
  • Evaluate on future data (temporal split, not random)

Resources:

  • FastAI Part 1: Practical Deep Learning (Highly recommended)
  • PyTorch tutorials
  • TensorFlow tutorials
  • Papers with Code (State-of-art implementations with code)

docs.title: Advanced Level: Deep Dive and Specialization
Learning Outcomes
docs.text:
After this section, you'll understand:

  • Advanced training techniques and optimization
  • Model compression and deployment
  • Specialized architectures (Vision Transformers, etc.)
  • Debugging and improving model performance

docs.title: Model Optimization
docs.text:
Quantization: Reduce precision (float32 → int8) for 4-8x memory/speed improvements with minimal accuracy loss.

Pruning: Remove unnecessary weights to reduce model size.

Knowledge Distillation: Train small model to mimic large model (small model gets large model's knowledge).

Mixed Precision Training: Use float16 for some operations, float32 for others (faster, same accuracy).

docs.title: Distributed Training
docs.text:
Training very large models across multiple GPUs or machines.

  • Data Parallelism: Split batch across GPUs (easy)
  • Model Parallelism: Split model across GPUs (complex)
  • Distributed frameworks: PyTorch DistributedDataParallel, TensorFlow distributed training

docs.title: Advanced Architectures
docs.text:
Vision Transformers (ViT): Apply transformers to images, often better than CNNs.

Diffusion Models: Generate images/text by iteratively denoising random noise.

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs): Learn on graph-structured data (molecules, social networks).

Multimodal Models: Learn from images + text simultaneously (CLIP, GPT-4V).

docs.title: Hyperparameter Tuning
docs.text:
Systematic approaches to finding best settings:

  • Grid Search: Try all combinations (slow)
  • Random Search: Sample random combinations (faster)
  • Bayesian Optimization: Use past results to guide search (smart)
  • Hyperband: Efficient resource allocation

docs.title: Debugging Deep Learning
docs.text:
Common issues and solutions:

Problem Likely Cause Solution Training loss doesn't decrease Learning rate too high Reduce learning rate Loss becomes NaN Numerical instability Reduce learning rate, check for bad data Slow training Small batch size or no GPU Increase batch size, use GPU Overfitting Model too large or insufficient regularization Add dropout, regularization, more data High validation loss Model underfitting Increase model capacity or training time

docs.title: Model Interpretability
docs.text:
Understanding what networks learn:

  • Visualize filters: What patterns do early layers detect?
  • Attention maps: Which input parts does model focus on?
  • Saliency maps: Which pixels are most important for prediction?
  • Tools: TorchVision, Captum, Grad-CAM

docs.title: Advanced Projects
docs.text:
Project 1: Custom Domain Application

  • Apply deep learning to your domain (medical imaging, satellite imagery, audio, etc.)
  • Experiment with transfer learning and fine-tuning
  • Deploy model as API

Project 2: Multi-task Learning

  • Train single model on multiple tasks (classification + localization)
  • More efficient than separate models

Project 3: Generative Model

  • Implement VAE or GAN to generate new images
  • Evaluate generation quality

Project 4: Production Deployment

  • Convert model to ONNX or TFLite format
  • Deploy as web service or mobile app
  • Monitor inference performance

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
Books:

  • Deep Learning by Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville (comprehensive, theory-heavy)
  • Practical Deep Learning for Coders by Jeremy Howard (practical, code-first)

Online Courses:

  • Stanford CS231N: CNN for Visual Recognition (Excellent, free)
  • Stanford CS224N: NLP with Deep Learning (Best for NLP)
  • Andrew Ng Deep Learning Specialization (Comprehensive)

Communities & Benchmarks:

  • Papers with Code - Latest research with implementations
  • Hugging Face Model Hub - Pre-trained models
  • Kaggle Competitions - Benchmark datasets

docs.title: Framework Choice: PyTorch vs. TensorFlow
docs.text:
Aspect PyTorch TensorFlow Learning Curve Easier, Pythonic Steeper, more verbose Debugging Better (eager execution) Harder (graph mode) Production Improving (TorchServe) Excellent (TensorFlow Serving) Community Research-focused Industry-focused Documentation Very good Good Performance Comparable Comparable

Recommendation: Start with PyTorch for learning, evaluate TensorFlow for production.

docs.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
docs.text:
1. Using test data during training → Data leakage, inflated performance
  2. Not scaling features → Poor convergence
  3. Training too long → Overfitting; use early stopping
  4. Poor batch size choice → Too small = slow; too large = poor convergence
  5. Not monitoring validation loss → Can't detect overfitting early
  6. Forgetting to normalize images → Unstable training
  7. Using wrong loss function → Wrong optimization direction
  8. Not saving checkpoints → Lose best model if training interrupted

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Start with Beginner level if you're new to deep learning
  2. Practice on MNIST, CIFAR-10 before custom datasets
  3. Move to Intermediate once basic networks work
  4. Specialize based on your interests:
  5. Computer Vision → Focus on CNNs and Vision Transformers
  6. NLP → Focus on Transformers and LLMs
  7. Time Series → Focus on RNNs and LSTMs
  8. Check Common Issues & Solutions if stuck

docs.title: Learning Resources
docs.text:
• PyTorch Official Tutorials
  • TensorFlow Documentation
  • StatQuest with Josh Starmer - Math fundamentals
  • Krish Naik - Practical deep learning
  • 3Blue1Brown Neural Networks Series - Beautiful intuition

docs.title: Math and Statistics
docs.text:
Once you are comfortable with the basics of deep learning and its libraries, understanding the mathematical foundations will help you:

  • Debug training issues (vanishing/exploding gradients)
  • Tune hyperparameters effectively
  • Design custom layers and loss functions

Refer to Math Essentials for:

  • Linear algebra review
  • Calculus for gradients
  • Probability and distributions

Additional resources:

  • StatQuest with Josh Starmer - Mathematical concepts explained simply
  • Krish Naik - Practical deep learning explanations

docs.title: AI/ML Glossary
docs.text: A quick reference for common terminology across the AI/ML learning path.

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text:
Activation Function - Non-linear function applied to neuron outputs (e.g., ReLU, sigmoid, tanh). Enables neural networks to learn non-linear patterns.

Backpropagation - Algorithm to compute gradients for training neural networks. Propagates error backwards through layers to update weights.

Batch - Subset of training data processed together. Helps with computational efficiency and gradient stability.

Bias - Offset parameter in neural networks. Allows models to fit data not passing through origin.

Classifier - Model that predicts discrete categories (e.g., spam/not spam, cat/dog/bird).

Clustering - Unsupervised learning task of grouping similar data points together.

Cross-Validation - Technique to evaluate model on different data splits to assess generalization.

Dataset - Collection of labeled or unlabeled examples used for training/testing.

Deep Learning - Machine learning using neural networks with multiple hidden layers.

Dimensionality Reduction - Technique to reduce number of features while preserving information (e.g., PCA).

Embedding - Dense vector representation of data that captures semantic meaning (e.g., word embeddings).

Epoch - One complete pass through entire training dataset.

Evaluation Metrics - Measures of model performance (e.g., accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).

Feature - Input variable used by model. Also called "attribute" or "predictor".

Feature Engineering - Process of creating new features from raw data to improve model performance.

Fine-tuning - Transfer learning technique where pre-trained model is further trained on new task.

Generalization - Model's ability to perform well on unseen data.

Gradient - Vector of partial derivatives; points in direction of steepest increase.

Gradient Descent - Optimization algorithm that iteratively updates parameters to minimize loss.

Ground Truth - Actual correct labels in dataset.

Hyperparameter - Parameter set before training (e.g., learning rate, batch size). Not learned from data.

Inference - Using trained model to make predictions on new data.

Label - Target output variable we're trying to predict.

Learning Rate - Hyperparameter controlling step size in gradient descent.

Loss Function - Function measuring prediction error. We minimize this during training.

Machine Learning - Creating models that learn patterns from data without explicit programming.

Model - Mathematical representation learned from data that makes predictions.

Neural Network - Computational model inspired by biological neurons, composed of interconnected nodes.

Optimization - Process of finding best model parameters by minimizing loss.

Overfitting - When model memorizes training data but fails on new data.

Parameter - Learnable weight or coefficient in model. Updated during training.

Preprocessing - Data cleaning and transformation before training (e.g., normalization, encoding).

Regression - Supervised learning task of predicting continuous numerical values.

Regularization - Technique to prevent overfitting (e.g., L1, L2, dropout).

Residual - Difference between predicted and actual value.

Supervised Learning - Learning from labeled examples (input-output pairs).

Test Set - Data used to evaluate final model performance. Must be unseen during training.

Training - Process of adjusting model parameters to minimize loss on training data.

Training Set - Data used to train model.

Transfer Learning - Using knowledge from one task to improve learning on another task.

Underfitting - When model is too simple and fails to capture patterns in data.

Unsupervised Learning - Learning from unlabeled data to find patterns or structure.

Validation Set - Data used during training to tune hyperparameters and monitor generalization.

Weight - Parameter in neural network connecting neurons. Learned during training.

docs.title: Deep Learning Specific
docs.text:
CNN (Convolutional Neural Network) - Neural network designed for image data using convolutional layers.

Convolution - Operation that slides a filter over input to extract local features.

Dropout - Regularization technique randomly disabling neurons during training to prevent overfitting.

LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) - Recurrent neural network variant that can learn long-term dependencies.

Pooling - Operation that downsamples feature maps by taking max or average of local regions.

RNN (Recurrent Neural Network) - Neural network with connections between time steps, good for sequences.

Transformer - Architecture using self-attention mechanism, foundation for modern language models.

Attention Mechanism - Technique allowing model to focus on relevant parts of input.

Self-Attention - Attention mechanism where sequence attends to itself.

docs.title: Generative AI Specific
docs.text:
Agent - System that can perceive environment, decide actions, and execute them to achieve goals.

Chain-of-Thought - Prompting technique where model explains reasoning step-by-step.

Context Window - Maximum number of tokens a model can process (e.g., GPT-4 has 8K or 32K tokens).

Fine-tuning - Further training of pre-trained LLM on specific task or domain data.

Hallucination - When LLM generates plausible but false information.

In-Context Learning - Learning from examples provided in prompt without fine-tuning.

LLM (Large Language Model) - Pre-trained neural network with billions of parameters, trained on large text corpora.

Prompt Engineering - Art of designing prompts to guide LLM toward desired outputs.

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) - Technique combining retrieval system with LLM to provide grounded answers.

Token - Basic unit text is split into (roughly 4 characters per token in English).

Temperature - Hyperparameter controlling randomness of LLM outputs (0 = deterministic, 1+ = more random).

Vector Database - Database storing dense embeddings for semantic search and retrieval.

Embedding Model - Model converting text/images to dense vectors capturing semantic meaning.

MCP (Model Context Protocol) - Protocol enabling AI models and tools to communicate standardized interfaces.

docs.title: Data Science Terms
docs.text:
Confusion Matrix - Table showing true positives, true negatives, false positives, false negatives.

Precision - Of predicted positives, how many were correct? (TP / (TP + FP))

Recall - Of actual positives, how many did we find? (TP / (TP + FN))

F1-Score - Harmonic mean of precision and recall; balanced metric for imbalanced datasets.

ROC Curve - Plot of true positive rate vs. false positive rate at different classification thresholds.

AUC (Area Under Curve) - Area under ROC curve; probability model ranks random positive higher than random negative.

Accuracy - Proportion of correct predictions out of all predictions.

MAE (Mean Absolute Error) - Average absolute difference between predictions and actual values.

RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) - Square root of average squared errors; penalizes large errors more.

Baseline - Simple model used as reference for comparing more complex models.

Confusion - Model incorrectly predicting one class as another.

docs.title: Optimization & Training
docs.text:
Batch Gradient Descent - Updates parameters using gradient computed on entire dataset.

Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) - Updates parameters using gradient computed on single example.

Mini-Batch Gradient Descent - Updates parameters using gradient computed on small batch.

Adam - Adaptive optimization algorithm combining momentum and adaptive learning rates.

Momentum - Optimization technique accumulating gradients over time for faster convergence.

Learning Rate Decay - Gradually reducing learning rate during training for convergence.

Convergence - Point where loss stops improving significantly, training can stop.

Vanishing Gradient - When gradients become too small during backpropagation, slowing learning.

Exploding Gradient - When gradients become too large, destabilizing training.

docs.title: Unsure about performance?
docs.text: See: Evaluation Metrics, Confusion Matrix, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, ROC Curve, Baseline

docs.text: See: Learning Rate, Vanishing Gradient, Exploding Gradient, Convergence, Overfitting, Underfitting, Regularization

docs.text: See: RNN, LSTM, Transformer, Token, Context Window, Embedding

docs.text: See: LLM, Prompt Engineering, In-Context Learning, RAG, Temperature, Hallucination, MCP

docs.text: See: Dimensionality Reduction, PCA, Embedding, Feature Engineering

docs.title: Math Essentials for AI/ML
docs.text: This guide covers the mathematical foundations essential for AI and Machine Learning. The focus is on intuition and application rather than rigorous proofs. You don't need to memorize formulas-understanding when and why they matter is what counts.

docs.title: Vectors and Matrices
docs.text:
Vectors are ordered lists of numbers. Think of them as points in space.

  • A 2D vector: [3, 5] represents a point at coordinates (3, 5)
  • A 3D vector: [1, 2, 3] represents a point in 3D space
  • In ML, vectors often represent features. For example, a house could be [price, size, bedrooms]

Matrices are grids of numbers-think of them as organized tables.

  • Used to store datasets: rows = samples, columns = features
  • Used to store model weights that transform data

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Concept Why It Matters Example Dot Product Measures similarity between vectors; fundamental to neural networks Two vectors with high dot product are "aligned" in the same direction Matrix Multiplication How data flows through neural networks Input × Weights = Output Inverse & Determinant Used in solving systems of equations and linear regression Solving Ax = b for x Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction Finding the most important directions in data Transpose Flips rows and columns; used in many ML algorithms Converting column vectors to row vectors

docs.title: Practical Implications
docs.text:
• Neural networks are essentially matrix operations chained together
  • Modern ML libraries (NumPy, PyTorch) handle the heavy computation; you focus on logic
  • Understanding matrix shapes helps debug dimension mismatches

docs.title: Derivatives and Gradients
docs.text:
A derivative measures how a function changes. In ML, we use derivatives to find where functions are increasing or decreasing.

Gradient is a vector of partial derivatives-it points in the direction of steepest increase.

Why this matters:

  • Model training is about finding weights that minimize error
  • We use gradients to move in the opposite direction (downhill) on an error landscape
  • This is called gradient descent

docs.title: Intuitive Example
docs.text:
Imagine you're in a fog on a hill:

  • The gradient tells you which direction is steepest uphill
  • To go down the hill (minimize error), you walk in the opposite direction
  • You take small steps (controlled by learning rate)
  • Eventually, you reach the bottom (optimal weights)

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Concept What It Does ML Context Derivative Measures rate of change of a function How error changes with respect to weights Chain Rule Computes derivatives of composite functions Backpropagation in neural networks Partial Derivative Derivative with respect to one variable at a time Updating individual weights Gradient Vector of all partial derivatives Direction to update weights during training Hessian Matrix Second-order derivatives Advanced optimization algorithms

docs.title: Practical Implications
docs.text:
• You rarely compute derivatives by hand in modern ML
  • Libraries like TensorFlow and PyTorch use automatic differentiation to compute gradients for you
  • Understanding the concept helps you debug training issues (e.g., "why isn't my loss decreasing?")

docs.title: Probability Distributions
docs.text:
A probability distribution describes the likelihood of different outcomes.

Why this matters:

  • Data rarely follows a single pattern; understanding distributions helps model real-world variability
  • Many ML algorithms assume specific distributions (e.g., Gaussian/Normal distribution)
  • Used to model uncertainty in predictions

docs.title: Key Distributions
docs.text: Distribution Shape Used For Example Normal (Gaussian) Bell curve Most common; assumed by many algorithms Heights, test scores, natural phenomena Uniform Flat line When all outcomes equally likely Random number generation Exponential Rapid drop Waiting times, event timing Time between customer arrivals Bernoulli Two outcomes Binary events (yes/no, 0/1) Coin flip, spam/not spam Categorical Multiple outcomes Multiple categories Dice roll, classification targets

docs.title: Statistics Concepts
docs.text: Concept What It Measures Why It Matters Mean Average value Central tendency; used as baseline Median Middle value Robust to outliers (unlike mean) Variance Spread of data High variance = more uncertainty Standard Deviation Square root of variance Same units as original data; easier to interpret Covariance How two variables change together Feature relationships; helps with correlation analysis Correlation Normalized covariance (-1 to 1) Strength of linear relationship between features

docs.title: Statistical Testing
docs.text:
• Confidence Intervals: Range where true value likely falls
  • Hypothesis Testing: Is the difference real or due to chance?
  • P-values: Probability of observing data if null hypothesis is true
  • Statistical Significance: When p < 0.05, we typically reject null hypothesis (convention)

docs.title: Practical Implications
docs.text:
• Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) uses statistics to understand your dataset
  • Feature scaling often assumes Normal distribution
  • Model evaluation uses statistical measures (mean absolute error, confidence intervals)

docs.title: Linear Regression: Putting It Together
docs.text:
Linear regression is a great example of how math concepts work together:

Problem: Find a line that best fits data points.

Math Setup:

  • We have data points: (x₁, y₁), (x₂, y₂), ..., (xₙ, yₙ)
  • We want to find line: y = mx + b
  • We need to minimize error (difference between predicted and actual y)

Solution Using Calculus:

  1. Define error function (e.g., sum of squared errors)
  2. Take derivative with respect to m and b
  3. Set derivatives to 0 (find minimum)
  4. Solve for m and b

Result: Using linear algebra, we can solve this directly:

weights = (X^T X)^(-1) X^T y

In Modern ML:

  • We don't solve directly; we use gradient descent
  • Compute gradient, move in opposite direction, repeat
  • Same result, but works for billions of parameters

docs.title: When You Need More Depth
docs.text:
The resources below go deeper into mathematical concepts:

  • Linear Algebra: 3Blue1Brown Essence of Linear Algebra
  • Calculus: 3Blue1Brown Essence of Calculus
  • Probability & Statistics: StatQuest with Josh Starmer - High quality, beginner-friendly
  • Math for ML: Mathematics for Machine Learning (Coursera)

docs.title: Key Takeaway
docs.text:
You don't need to be a mathematician to excel in AI/ML. Focus on:

  • Intuition over memorization
  • Practice with real data
  • When to apply concepts rather than deriving them
  • Let libraries handle the heavy computation

Modern frameworks abstract away complex math, but understanding these foundations helps you make better decisions about models, debug training issues, and avoid common pitfalls.

docs.title: Learning Paths by Goal
docs.text: This document helps you choose your learning path based on what you want to build. All paths assume you have the Prerequisites covered.

docs.title: 1. Build Chatbots & Conversational AI
docs.text:
Your goal: Create an AI assistant that can hold conversations, answer questions, or provide support.

Learning Path:

  1. Start with basics → Generative AI Fundamentals
  2. Understand transformers and LLMs
  3. Learn how language models work

  4. Time: 1-2 weeks

  5. Learn Prompt Engineering → Generative AI - Intermediate

  6. Master prompt techniques (few-shot, chain-of-thought, role-playing)
  7. Experiment with free LLM APIs

  8. Time: 1 week

  9. Add Memory & Context → Generative AI - Advanced

  10. Learn LangChain for conversation management
  11. Implement memory systems (conversation history)

  12. Time: 1-2 weeks

  13. Optional: Ground with Real Data → RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)

  14. Add vector databases to retrieve relevant context
  15. Make your chatbot knowledgeable about specific documents

  16. Time: 1 week

  17. Deploy → Production Deployment (Coming Soon)

  18. Host your chatbot as an API or web service

Minimal viable project timeline: 3-4 weeks (skip optional RAG initially)

Key Skills: Prompt engineering, LLM APIs, basic LangChain

docs.title: 2. Build Recommendation Systems
docs.text:
Your goal: Create systems that recommend products, content, or services to users.

Learning Path:

  1. Understand ML Fundamentals → Machine Learning Basics
  2. Supervised learning concepts
  3. Model evaluation metrics

  4. Time: 2 weeks

  5. Learn Key Algorithms → Machine Learning - Algorithms (Deep-dive)

  6. Collaborative filtering
  7. Content-based filtering
  8. Similarity measures (cosine similarity, Euclidean distance)

  9. Time: 1-2 weeks

  10. Optional: Use Embeddings → Embeddings & Vectors

  11. Semantic similarity using embeddings
  12. Vector databases for fast retrieval

  13. Time: 1 week

  14. Practice on Real Data → Kaggle datasets or competitions

  15. Time: 2-3 weeks

  16. Deploy → Production Deployment (Coming Soon)

Minimal viable project timeline: 6-8 weeks

Key Skills: Collaborative filtering, similarity metrics, vector databases

docs.title: 3. Build Computer Vision Applications
docs.text:
Your goal: Create systems that understand and process images (object detection, classification, segmentation).

Learning Path:

  1. Understand Deep Learning Basics → Deep Learning Fundamentals
  2. Core concepts (neurons, activation functions, loss)

  3. Time: 1 week

  4. Learn Neural Network Fundamentals → Deep Learning Hands-on Basics

  5. Build simple neural networks with PyTorch

  6. Time: 1 week

  7. Learn CNNs (Convolutional Neural Networks) → Deep Learning - CNNs (Coming Soon)

  8. How convolutions work
  9. Architecture designs (AlexNet, ResNet, etc.)

  10. Time: 2 weeks

  11. Practice on Datasets → Deep Learning

  12. CIFAR-10, ImageNet, custom datasets

  13. Time: 2-3 weeks

  14. Advanced: Transfer Learning → Deep Learning - Intermediate

  15. Use pre-trained models for faster results
  16. Fine-tune on your specific problem

  17. Time: 1-2 weeks

  18. Deploy → Production Deployment (Coming Soon)

Minimal viable project timeline: 7-9 weeks

Key Skills: CNNs, transfer learning, image preprocessing

docs.title: 4. Build Natural Language Processing Applications
docs.text:
Your goal: Create systems that understand and generate human language (sentiment analysis, summarization, translation, etc.).

Learning Path:

  1. Understand Deep Learning Basics → Deep Learning - Beginner
  2. Core concepts

  3. Time: 1 week

  4. Learn RNNs & Sequences → Deep Learning - RNNs/LSTMs (Coming Soon)

  5. How RNNs process sequences
  6. LSTMs and GRUs for long-term dependencies

  7. Time: 1-2 weeks

  8. Understand Transformers → Transformers Introduction

  9. Attention mechanism
  10. Modern transformer architecture

  11. Time: 1-2 weeks

  12. Use Pre-trained NLP Models → Gen AI - Beginner/Intermediate

  13. Hugging Face Transformers library
  14. BERT, GPT models
  15. Fine-tuning for your task

  16. Time: 1-2 weeks

  17. Advanced: Large Language Models → Generative AI - Advanced

  18. Working with LLMs directly
  19. Prompt engineering

  20. Time: 1-2 weeks

  21. Deploy → Production Deployment (Coming Soon)

Minimal viable project timeline: 6-8 weeks

Key Skills: Transformers, LLMs, Hugging Face, prompt engineering

docs.title: 5. Build Autonomous Agents
docs.text:
Your goal: Create intelligent agents that can plan, decide actions, and interact with tools and APIs.

Learning Path:

  1. Strong Generative AI Foundation → Generative AI - Full Path
  2. Understand LLMs deeply

  3. Time: 4-5 weeks

  4. Learn Agent Concepts → Agents

  5. Agent architectures (single, hierarchical, supervisor)
  6. When to use agents vs. RAG

  7. Time: 1 week

  8. Learn Tool Use & Function Calling → Agents - Advanced (Deep-dive)

  9. How agents call external tools

  10. Time: 1 week

  11. Optional: Model Context Protocol → MCP

  12. Standardized way to expose tools/data to agents

  13. Time: 1 week

  14. Build & Test → Create multi-step agent workflows

  15. Time: 2-3 weeks

  16. Deploy → Production Deployment (Coming Soon)

Minimal viable project timeline: 8-10 weeks

Key Skills: LLMs, agent frameworks (LangGraph), tool use, planning

docs.title: 6. Full ML/AI Engineering Career Path
docs.text:
Your goal: Become a generalist who can handle any AI/ML task.

Learning Path:

This is the most comprehensive path. Follow this sequence:

docs.title: Foundation (4-6 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Math Essentials - Refresher on linear algebra, calculus, probability
  2. Prerequisites - Python, Jupyter, environment setup
  3. Glossary - Reference as you learn

docs.title: Machine Learning (6-8 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Machine Learning Fundamentals
  2. Practice: 2-3 small projects on Kaggle
  3. Machine Learning - Algorithms Deep-dive (Coming Soon)

docs.title: Deep Learning (8-10 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Deep Learning - Beginner
  2. Deep Learning - Intermediate
  3. Practice: Image classification and NLP projects
  4. Deep Learning - Advanced (Coming Soon)

docs.title: Generative AI & Modern Applications (6-8 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Generative AI - Complete Path
  2. RAG
  3. Agents
  4. Practice: Build 2-3 generative AI applications

docs.title: Production & Deployment (2-3 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Production Deployment (Coming Soon)
  2. Learn Docker, FastAPI, cloud deployment
  3. Practice: Deploy one model to production

docs.title: Troubleshooting & Debugging (Ongoing)
docs.text:
• Reference Common Issues & Solutions as needed

Total timeline: 4-5 months

Key Skills: Everything! ML fundamentals, deep learning, generative AI, production deployment

docs.title: How to Use This Roadmap
docs.text:
1. Find your goal above
  2. Note the path and timeline
  3. Start at the first link and proceed in order
  4. Do practice projects at each stage (don't just read)
  5. Refer to Glossary when you encounter unfamiliar terms
  6. Check Common Issues if stuck
  7. Come back here after one path to learn a new skill

docs.title: Still Deciding?
docs.text:
If you want immediate results (1-3 months): → Go for Path #1 (Chatbots) or #2 (Recommendations)

If you have time to invest (3-5 months): → Follow Path #6 (Full ML/AI Engineering)

If you want to specialize early: → Pick Path #3 (Computer Vision), #4 (NLP), or #5 (Agents)

If unsure: → Start with Machine Learning Basics, then decide based on your interest

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Pick your path
  2. Read the Prerequisites if you haven't already
  3. Go to the first link in your chosen path
  4. Start learning!

Questions? Check Common Issues & Solutions or refer to the Glossary.

docs.title: Generative AI Learning Path
docs.text: This document outlines a structured learning path for mastering Generative AI, covering essential concepts, tools, and techniques. The path is divided into beginner, intermediate, and advanced levels, with each section building on the previous one.

docs.title: Pre-requisites
docs.text:
Before diving into Generative AI, ensure you have a understanding of the following concepts:

  • Python and pip package management
  • Google Colab or Jupyter Notebooks

docs.title: Beginner Level
docs.text:
• Familiarity with machine learning concepts like training, testing, and evaluation metrics.
  • Understanding of how neural networks work.
  • Natural Language Processing (NLP) fundamentals.
  • Using Hugging Face Transformers library.
  • Tokenization and text preprocessing techniques.
  • Understanding of vectors and similarity measures.
  • Understanding of word embeddings.

docs.title: Free LLM APIS
docs.text:
• Together AI

    • Go to models page and search for free models.
    • You also get $5 free credits to use on their platform.

  • Groq

    • Offers some level of free usage.

  • Google Gemini

    • Offers free access to some of their Gemini models.

  • If you have good GPU on you local machine, you can use Llama.cpp.

    • Its good for prototyping and testing LLM code locally but do not expect high performance.

docs.title: Courses
docs.text: • https://developers.google.com/machine-learning/crash-course

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
1. Text Classification with Transformers
  2. Build a text classification model using Hugging Face Transformers for sentiment analysis.

  3. Try experimenting with different pre-trained models.

  4. Experiment with LLM APIs

    • Use free LLM APIs to generate text based on prompts.
    • Compare the outputs of different models.
    • Experiment with different prompt structures to see how they affect the generated text.

docs.title: Intermediate Level
docs.text:
• Understanding of LLMs (Large Language Models)
  • Prompt engineering techniques.
  • RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) concepts.
  • Vector databases and their applications (FAISS, Pinecone, Qdrant).
  • Text extraction from various formats (PDF, DOCX, Image) using pdfplumber, pytesseract.
  • LangChain framework.

docs.title: Courses
docs.text:
• Daily Dose of Data Science I highly recommend this blog for practical AI skills. This is a paid resource but worth the investment. If possible subscribe for Lifetime membership.
  • LLM Course by Hugging Face
  • LangChain Mastery in 2025 | Full 5 Hour Course LangChain v0.3
  • Complete Gen AI Langchain - This is paid Udemy course. The course is 54hrs, but start from "Intro to GenAI module"

docs.title: Vector DBs
docs.text:
• Pinecone
    • Free tier available with limited usage.
  • Qdrant
    • Docker image available for local setup.
  • FAISS
    • Available as a library for local use.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
1. RAG Implementation

    • Implement a RAG system using a combination of retrieval and generation techniques.

  2. Document Summarization

    • Develop a document summarization tool using LLMs.

  3. Vector Database Exploration

    • Explore the use of vector databases for storing and retrieving embeddings.
    • Implement a simple application that uses a vector database for semantic search.

docs.title: Advanced Level
docs.text:
• Memory Management in LangChain.
  • LangGraph for building complex workflows using agents.
  • Model Context Protocol.
  • OCR (Optical Character Recognition) techniques.

docs.title: What Are Agents?
docs.text:
Agents are autonomous systems that use LLMs to understand tasks, make decisions, and take actions. Unlike simple chatbots that respond to questions, agents can:

  • Break down complex problems into steps
  • Call external tools (search, calculate, send emails, query databases)
  • Reason about results and decide next steps
  • Iterate until the goal is achieved

docs.title: Real-World Examples
docs.text: Use Case What the Agent Does Research Assistant Researches topic, finds sources, synthesizes findings into report Customer Support Gathers customer info, checks knowledge base, accesses systems to resolve issue Code Assistant Understands requirements, searches documentation, writes and tests code Financial Advisor Gathers market data, calculates scenarios, recommends strategies

docs.title: Agents vs. RAG vs. Fine-tuning
docs.text:
Approach When to Use Limitation Simple LLM General questions, simple tasks No tool access; can hallucinate RAG Question-answering over documents Passive retrieval; no multi-step reasoning Fine-tuning Specific domain knowledge Expensive, requires retraining for new info Agents Complex multi-step tasks, tool use More complex to implement

Decision: Use Agents when you need:

  • Multiple steps to solve a problem
  • Access to external tools (APIs, databases, search)
  • Reasoning and decision-making
  • Adaptive behavior based on intermediate results

docs.title: The Agent Loop
docs.text:
1. Observe: Agent receives task and available tools
  2. Think: LLM reasons about task and decides which tool to use
  3. Act: Agent calls the tool
  4. Reflect: LLM evaluates result
  5. Repeat: If goal not reached, loop back to step 2
  6. Respond: Return final answer to user

docs.title: Example: Research Agent
docs.text:
User: "Find info about latest AI trends and write a summary"

Step 1 (Think): "I need to search for latest AI trends"
Step 2 (Act): Call search_web("AI trends 2025")
Step 3 (Reflect): Got 5 articles. Need more specific info.
Step 4 (Act): Call search_web("large language models advances 2025")
Step 5 (Reflect): Have enough info. Now write summary.
Step 6 (Act): Write comprehensive summary from search results
Step 7 (Respond): Return summary to user

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:

  • Know agent types and architectures
  • Recognize tool use and decision-making

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text:
Tools: External functions agents can call (search, calculator, email, API, etc.)

Tool Calling/Function Calling: LLM can decide which tool to use and with what parameters.

Tool Calling Flow:

LLM: "I'll use the search_web tool with query='AI trends'"
System: Executes search_web('AI trends')
LLM: Sees results, decides next action

docs.title: 1. Single Agent
docs.text:
Simple agent with fixed set of tools.

User → LLM Agent → Tools (search, calculator, etc.) → Response

Use: Simple tasks with clear steps.

docs.title: 2. Supervisor Agent (Multi-agent)
docs.text:
Central "supervisor" coordinates multiple specialist agents.

         ┌─ Analyst Agent (analyzes data)
User → Supervisor Agent ├─ Researcher Agent (finds info)
         └─ Writer Agent (writes report)

Use: Complex tasks requiring different specialists.

docs.title: 3. Hierarchical Agents
docs.text:
Tree structure with management levels.

              CEO
          /    |    \
    Research   IT   Writing
      /  \    / \    / \
    Data Market Dev Infra Tech  Summary

Use: Very complex problems requiring delegation.

docs.title: Basic Agent Structure
docs.text:
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.agents import create_react_agent, AgentExecutor
from langchain.tools import Tool

# Define tools
def search_web(query):
    # Your search implementation
    return "Search results..."

tools = [
    Tool(
        name="search_web",
        func=search_web,
        description="Search the web for information"
    )
]

# Create agent
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4")
agent = create_react_agent(llm, tools)
executor = AgentExecutor.from_agent_and_tools(
    agent=agent, 
    tools=tools, 
    verbose=True
)

# Use agent
result = executor.invoke({"input": "Research latest AI trends"})

Task: "Conduct market research and write a report"

The supervisor:

  1. Routes task to Researcher (finds information)
  2. Routes results to Analyst (analyzes findings)
  3. Routes insights to Writer (writes report)
  4. Compiles final response

Tool Validation:

Cost Optimization:

Monitoring & Logging:

docs.title: Common Challenges & Solutions
docs.text: Challenge Solution Agent gets stuck in loop Add max iterations, better error handling Tool gives irrelevant results Improve tool descriptions, add input validation Hallucination (making up tool results) Use real tools only, validate outputs Slow execution Parallel tool calls, caching, cheaper models High costs Smaller models, fewer tool calls, batching

docs.title: When NOT to Use Agents
docs.text:
• Simple Q&A - Use simple LLM or RAG

  • Guaranteed accuracy needed - Agents can make mistakes
  • No external data - Use fine-tuning instead

docs.title: What Is MCP?
docs.text: Model Context Protocol is a standardized way for AI models and tools to communicate with each other.

docs.title: The Problem MCP Solves
docs.text:
Before MCP:

  • Each AI tool/agent had its own custom way to call functions
  • No standardization - messy, hard to integrate
  • Lots of duplicate code for similar functionality

With MCP:

  • Standard protocol everyone follows
  • Tools can be easily swapped
  • Once you learn it, works everywhere

docs.title: Real-World Analogy
docs.text:
Think of MCP like HTTP for AI tools:

  • Without HTTP: Every website has its own communication protocol
  • With HTTP: All websites use the same protocol
  • Same with MCP: All AI tools use same protocol

docs.title: MCP Use Cases
docs.text:
• AI Assistants accessing company databases or APIs
  • Agents using standard tools for web search, calculations, etc.
  • LLMs connecting to external data sources
  • Multi-tool workflows where tools need to communicate

docs.title: MCP Components
docs.text:
MCP Server:

  • Exposes tools and data sources
  • Runs your custom code
  • Provides standardized interface

MCP Client:

  • LLM or agent using the MCP server
  • Makes tool calls through MCP protocol
  • Gets results back

MCP Tools:

  • Functions/capabilities server exposes
  • Examples: search, calculate, query database, send email

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:
• Understand what MCP is and why it matters
  • Know how MCP differs from alternatives
  • Recognize MCP architecture

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text:
Protocol: Set of rules for how two systems communicate.

Server: System that provides tools/services (implements MCP).

Client: System that uses the tools (LLM, Agent).

Tool: Function/capability exposed by MCP server.

docs.title: Why Use MCP?
docs.text: Aspect Without MCP With MCP Integration Custom code for each tool Standard protocol Learning curve Different for each tool Learn once, use everywhere Switching tools Rewrite code Just swap the server Maintenance Lots of custom logic Standardized maintenance

docs.title: MCP vs. Other Approaches
docs.text: Approach Use Case Pros Cons Direct API calls Quick prototype Simple No standard, hard to scale Webhooks Event-driven Flexible Complex setup OpenAI Function Calling OpenAI-specific Easy Locked to one provider MCP Multiple tools, standards Interoperable, standard Newer, less adoption

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:
• Build a working MCP server
  • Expose tools through MCP
  • Connect agent to MCP server

docs.title: Creating an MCP Server
docs.text:
An MCP server exposes tools that clients (LLMs, agents) can call.

Basic Structure:

from fastmcp import FastMCP

# Create server
mcp = FastMCP("My Service")

# Define a tool
@mcp.tool()
async def my_tool(param: str) -> str:
    """Tool description."""
    return "Tool result"

# Run server
if __name__ == "__main__":
    mcp.run(transport="streamable-http")

docs.title: Example: Web Search MCP Server
docs.text:
from fastmcp import FastMCP
from tavily import TavilyClient
import os
import logging

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

mcp = FastMCP("Search Service")
tavily_client = TavilyClient(api_key=os.getenv("TAVILY_API_KEY"))

@mcp.tool()
async def search_web(query: str) -> str:
    """Search the web for information about the query.

    Args:
        query: The search query

    Returns:
        Search results with titles, URLs, and summaries
    """
    logger.info(f"Searching for: {query}")
    response = tavily_client.search(query, limit=5)
    results = response.get("results", [])

    formatted_results = []
    for item in results:
        formatted_results.append(
            f"Title: {item['title']}\n"
            f"URL: {item['url']}\n"
            f"Content: {item['content'][:500]}..."
        )

    return "\n\n".join(formatted_results)

@mcp.tool()
async def extract_facts(content: str) -> str:
    """Extract key facts from the given content.

    Args:
        content: Text to extract facts from

    Returns:
        Bullet-point list of key facts
    """
    # You could use an LLM here for more sophisticated extraction
    sentences = content.split(". ")
    facts = [f"• {s.strip()}" for s in sentences[:5] if s.strip()]
    return "\n".join(facts)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    mcp.run(transport="streamable-http")

docs.title: Using MCP Server from Agent
docs.text:
from langchain_mcp_adapters.client import MultiServerMCPClient
from langchain.chat_models import init_chat_model

# Connect to MCP server
client = MultiServerMCPClient(
    {
        "search_service": {
            "url": "http://localhost:8000/mcp/",
            "transport": "streamable_http",
        }
    }
)

# Get available tools
mcp_tools = await client.get_tools()

# Create LLM with tools
llm = init_chat_model("gpt-4")
llm_with_tools = llm.bind_tools(mcp_tools)

# Use in agent
from langchain.agents import create_react_agent, AgentExecutor

agent = create_react_agent(llm_with_tools, mcp_tools)
executor = AgentExecutor.from_agent_and_tools(agent, mcp_tools)
result = executor.invoke({"input": "Search for latest AI news"})

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Clear tool descriptions: Helps LLM understand when to use each tool
  2. Type hints: Specify input/output types precisely
  3. Error handling: Catch and return meaningful error messages
  4. Logging: Log tool calls for debugging
  5. Performance: Keep tool responses quick

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:

  • Handle errors and edge cases

  • Integrate with multiple tools

docs.title: Advanced Concepts
docs.text:
Multi-tool Server:

mcp = FastMCP("Multi-Service Hub")

@mcp.tool()
async def search_web(query: str) -> str:
    # Web search implementation
    pass

@mcp.tool()
async def query_database(sql: str) -> str:
    # Database query implementation
    pass

@mcp.tool()
async def send_email(to: str, subject: str, body: str) -> str:
    # Email sending implementation
    pass

Error Handling:

@mcp.tool()
async def risky_tool(param: str) -> str:
    try:
        result = perform_risky_operation(param)
        return result
    except ValueError as e:
        return f"Invalid input: {str(e)}"
    except Exception as e:
        logger.error(f"Tool error: {e}")
        return f"Tool failed: {str(e)}"

Async Operations:

import asyncio

@mcp.tool()
async def parallel_search(queries: list[str]) -> str:
    """Search multiple queries in parallel."""
    tasks = [
        search_web(query)
        for query in queries
    ]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    return "\n\n".join(results)

@mcp.tool()
async def query_products(category: str, min_price: int) -> str:

    query = """
        SELECT name, price FROM products
        WHERE category = ? AND price >= ?
    """
    results = db.execute(query, (category, min_price))

@mcp.tool()
async def convert_document(format_from: str, format_to: str, content: str) -> str:
    """Convert document between formats."""

    result = transformation_library.convert(content, format_from, format_to)

docs.title: When to Use MCP
docs.text:
Use MCP when:

  • Multiple tools need standard interface
  • Building enterprise system with many integrations
  • Want interoperability between different LLMs
  • Need standardized tool discovery and management

Don't use MCP when:

  • One-off prototype
  • Simple API wrapper
  • Performance is critical (adds slight overhead)
  • Requires real-time communication

docs.title: Production Deployment Guide
docs.text: This guide covers taking your AI/ML models from development to production deployment.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Deploying AI models to production involves:

  • Model serialization (saving trained models)
  • API creation (exposing model as service)
  • Containerization (Docker for consistency)
  • Cloud hosting (AWS, GCP, Azure, etc.)
  • Monitoring & scaling (track performance, handle load)
  • Cost optimization (efficient resource usage)

docs.title: Pre-deployment Checklist
docs.text:
Before deploying, ensure:

  • [ ] Model training is complete and reproducible
  • [ ] Model performance validated on test set
  • [ ] Dependencies documented (requirements.txt)
  • [ ] Code tested locally
  • [ ] Security considerations addressed (API keys, data protection)
  • [ ] Performance benchmarks established (latency, throughput)
  • [ ] Rollback plan exists
  • [ ] Monitoring/logging setup planned

docs.title: Model Serialization
docs.text:
Save your trained model for later loading:

PyTorch:

import torch

# Save model
torch.save(model.state_dict(), 'model.pth')

# Load model
model = MyModel()  # Define architecture
model.load_state_dict(torch.load('model.pth'))
model.eval()  # Set to evaluation mode

TensorFlow:

import tensorflow as tf

# Save model
model.save('model_directory')  # SavedModel format

# Load model
model = tf.keras.models.load_model('model_directory')

Scikit-learn:

import pickle

# Save model
with open('model.pkl', 'wb') as f:
    pickle.dump(model, f)

# Load model
with open('model.pkl', 'rb') as f:
    model = pickle.load(f)

docs.text:
Create requirements file for reproducibility:

pip freeze > requirements.txt

docs.title: Why APIs?
docs.text:
APIs allow client applications to use your model without running it locally.

┌─────────────────┐
│ Client App      │
│ (Web, Mobile)   │
└────────┬────────┘
         │ HTTP/REST
         │
    ┌────▼────────┐
    │ API Server  │
    │ (FastAPI)   │
    └────┬────────┘
         │
    ┌────▼────────┐

    │ (PyTorch)   │
    └─────────────┘

docs.title: FastAPI: Building ML APIs
docs.text:
Installation:

pip install fastapi uvicorn python-multipart

Simple Prediction API:

from fastapi import FastAPI, File, UploadFile
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
import torch
import numpy as np
from PIL import Image
import io

app = FastAPI(title="Image Classification API", version="1.0")

# Load model at startup
model = None

@app.on_event("startup")
async def load_model():
    global model
    model = torch.jit.load('model.pth')
    model.eval()

@app.get("/health")
async def health_check():
    """Health check endpoint."""
    return {"status": "healthy"}

@app.post("/predict")
async def predict(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
    """Make prediction on uploaded image."""
    try:
        # Read image
        contents = await file.read()
        image = Image.open(io.BytesIO(contents))

        # Preprocess
        image = image.resize((224, 224))
        image_array = np.array(image) / 255.0
        image_tensor = torch.from_numpy(image_array).permute(2, 0, 1).unsqueeze(0).float()

        # Predict
        with torch.no_grad():
            output = model(image_tensor)
            prediction = torch.argmax(output, dim=1).item()
            confidence = torch.softmax(output, dim=1)[0][prediction].item()

        return {
            "prediction": prediction,
            "confidence": float(confidence),
            "status": "success"
        }
    except Exception as e:
        return {"error": str(e), "status": "error"}, 500

@app.post("/batch_predict")
async def batch_predict(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
    """Make predictions on batch of images."""
    # Implementation for multiple images
    pass

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)

Run locally:

python app.py
# Access at http://localhost:8000
# Docs at http://localhost:8000/docs

docs.title: API Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Input validation:
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class PredictionRequest(BaseModel):
    text: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=5000)
    confidence_threshold: float = Field(0.5, ge=0, le=1)
  1. Error handling:
from fastapi import HTTPException

@app.post("/predict")
async def predict(request: PredictionRequest):
    try:
        result = model.predict(request.text)
        return {"result": result}
    except ValueError as e:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail=str(e))
    except Exception as e:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail="Internal error")
  1. Rate limiting:
pip install slowapi
  1. Caching:
from functools import lru_cache

@lru_cache(maxsize=100)
def get_cached_prediction(input_text: str):
    return model.predict(input_text)

docs.title: Why Docker?
docs.text:
Docker ensures your model runs identically everywhere:

  • Local machine
  • Staging server
  • Production cloud

docs.title: Dockerfile Example
docs.text:
# Base image with Python
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Copy requirements
COPY requirements.txt .

# Install dependencies
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code
COPY app.py .
COPY model.pth .

# Expose port
EXPOSE 8000

# Health check
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=5s --retries=3 \
    CMD python -c "import requests; requests.get('http://localhost:8000/health')"

# Run application
CMD ["uvicorn", "app:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
